subject: Physics
question_text: Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –7 m 2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. Assume that each copper atom contributes roughly one conduction electron. The density of copper is 9.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 3 kg/m 3 , and its atomic mass is 63.5 u. Compare the drift speed obtained above with, thermal speeds of copper atoms at ordinary temperatures, speed of propagation of electric field along the conductor which causes the drift motion.
question_html:
1. Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10–7 m2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. Assume that each copper atom contributes roughly one conduction electron. The density of copper is 9.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 103 kg/m3, and its atomic mass is 63.5 u.
  2. Compare the drift speed obtained above with,
    1. thermal speeds of copper atoms at ordinary temperatures,
    2. speed of propagation of electric field along the conductor which causes the drift motion.
solution_text: The direction of drift velocity of conduction electrons is opposite to the electric field direction, i.e., electrons drift in the direction of increasing potential. The drift speed v d is given by Equation ,v d = (I/neA) Now, e = 1.6 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –19 C, A = 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –7 m 2 , I = 1.5 A. The density of n conduction electrons, is equal to the number of atoms per cubic metre (assuming one conduction electron per Cu atom as is reasonable from its valence electron count of one). A cubic metre of copper has a mass of 9.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 3 kg. Since 6.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 23 copper atoms have a mass of 63.5 g, {tex}n=\frac{6.0 \times 10^{23}}{63.5} \times 9.0 \times 10^{6}{/tex} number of the conduction electron per unit volume is ,n= 8.5 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 28 m –3 which gives, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{1.5}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –3 m s –1 = 1.1 mm s –1 At a temperature T, the thermal speed* of a copper atom of mass M is obtained from <( {tex}\frac 12{/tex} ) Mv 2 > = (3/2) k B T ] and is thus typical of the order of {tex}\sqrt{k_{B} T / M}{/tex} (relation between velocity and temperature can be find by this relation) , where k B is the Boltzmann constant. For copper at 300 K, this is about 2 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 2 m/s. There will be the random vibrational speeds of copper atoms in a conductor. Note that the drift speed of electrons is much smaller, about 10 –5 times the typical thermal speed at ordinary temperatures. An electric field travelling along the conductor has a speed of an electromagnetic wave, namely equal to 3.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 8 m s –1 . The drift speed is, in comparison, extremely small; smaller by a factor of 10 –11 .(speed must be less than speed of light)
solution_html:
1. The direction of drift velocity of conduction electrons is opposite to the electric field direction, i.e., electrons drift in the direction of increasing potential. The drift speed vd is given by Equation ,vd = (I/neA) Now, e = 1.6 {tex}\times {/tex} 10–19 C, A = 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10–7m2, I = 1.5 A. The density of  n conduction electrons,  is equal to the number of atoms per cubic metre (assuming one conduction electron per Cu atom as is reasonable from its valence electron count of one). A cubic metre of copper has a mass of 9.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 103kg. Since 6.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 1023 copper atoms have a mass of 63.5 g,
    {tex}n=\frac{6.0 \times 10^{23}}{63.5} \times 9.0 \times 10^{6}{/tex}
    number of the conduction electron per unit volume is ,n= 8.5 {tex}\times {/tex} 1028 m–3
    which gives,
    {tex}v_{d}=\frac{1.5}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex}
    = 1.1 {tex}\times {/tex} 10–3 m s–1 = 1.1 mm s–1
  2.  
    1. At a temperature T, the thermal speed* of a copper atom of mass M is obtained from <({tex}\frac 12{/tex}) Mv2 > = (3/2) kBT ] and is thus typical of the order of {tex}\sqrt{k_{B} T / M}{/tex} (relation between velocity and temperature can be find by this relation) , where kB is the Boltzmann constant. For copper at 300 K, this is about 2 {tex}\times {/tex} 102 m/s. There will be  the random vibrational speeds of copper atoms in a conductor. Note that the drift speed of electrons is much smaller, about 10–5 times the typical thermal speed at ordinary temperatures.
    2. An electric field travelling along the conductor has a speed of an electromagnetic wave, namely equal to 3.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 108 m s–1. The drift speed is, in comparison, extremely small; smaller by a factor of 10–11.(speed must be less than speed of light)

subject: Physics
question_text: In Example 3.1, the electron drift speed is estimated to be only a few mm s –1 for currents in the range of a few amperes? How then is current established almost instant a circuit is closed? The electron drift arises due to the force experienced by electrons in the electric field inside the conductor. But force should cause acceleration. Why then do the electrons acquire a steady average drift speed? If the electron drift speed is so small, and the electron’s charge is small, how can we still obtain large amounts of current in a conductor? When electrons drift in metal from lower to higher potential, does it mean that all the free electrons of the metal are moving in the same direction? Are the paths of electrons straight lines between successive collisions (with the positive ions of the metal) in the absence of electric field, presence of electric field?
question_html:
1. In Example 3.1, the electron drift speed is estimated to be only a few mm s–1 for currents in the range of a few amperes? How then is current established almost instant a circuit is closed?
  2. The electron drift arises due to the force experienced by electrons in the electric field inside the conductor. But force should cause acceleration. Why then do the electrons acquire a steady average drift speed?
  3. If the electron drift speed is so small, and the electron’s charge is small, how can we still obtain large amounts of current in a conductor?
  4. When electrons drift in metal from lower to higher potential, does it mean that all the free electrons of the metal are moving in the same direction?
  5. Are the paths of electrons straight lines between successive collisions (with the positive ions of the metal) in the
    1. absence of electric field,
    2. presence of electric field?
solution_text: Electric field is established throughout the circuit, almost instantly (with the speed of light) causing at every point a local electron drift. Establishment of current does not have to wait for electrons from one end of the conductor travelling to the other end. Each electron is accelerated due to the effect of the electric field until it collides with a positive ion of the metal and loses its drift speed. However, it does take a little while for the current to reach its steady value. Each ‘free’ electron does accelerate, increasing its drift speed until it collides with a positive ion of the metal. It loses its drift speed after the collision but starts to accelerate and increases its drift speed again only to suffer a collision again and so on. On average, therefore, electrons acquire only a drift speed. Simple, because the electron number density is enormous, {tex}\sim{/tex} 10 29 m –3 . By no means, the drift velocity is superposed over the large random velocities of electrons. In the absence of an electric field, the paths are straight lines; in the presence of an electric field, the paths are, in general, curved.
solution_html:
1. Electric field is established throughout the circuit, almost instantly (with the speed of light) causing at every point a local electron drift. Establishment of current does not have to wait for electrons from one end of the conductor travelling to the other end. Each electron is accelerated due to the effect of the electric field until it collides with a positive ion of the metal and loses its drift speed. However, it does take a little while for the current to reach its steady value.
  2. Each ‘free’ electron does accelerate, increasing its drift speed until it collides with a positive ion of the metal. It loses its drift speed after the collision but starts to accelerate and increases its drift speed again only to suffer a collision again and so on. On average, therefore, electrons acquire only a drift speed.
  3. Simple, because the electron number density is enormous, {tex}\sim{/tex}1029 m–3.
  4. By no means, the drift velocity is superposed over the large random velocities of electrons.
  5. In the absence of an electric field, the paths are straight lines; in the presence of an electric field, the paths are, in general, curved.

subject: Physics
question_text: An electric toaster uses nichrome for its heating element. When a negligibly small current passes through it, its resistance at room temperature (27.0 °C) is found to be 75.3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . When the toaster is connected to a 230 V supply, the current settles, after a few seconds, to a steady value of 2.68 A. What is the steady temperature of the nichrome element? The temperature coefficient of resistance of nichrome averaged over the temperature range involved, is 1.70 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –4 °C –1 .
question_html: An electric toaster uses nichrome for its heating element. When a negligibly small current passes through it, its resistance at room temperature (27.0 °C) is found to be 75.3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. When the toaster is connected to a 230 V supply, the current settles, after a few seconds, to a steady value of 2.68 A. What is the steady temperature of the nichrome element? The temperature coefficient of resistance of nichrome averaged over the temperature range involved, is 1.70 {tex}\times {/tex} 10–4 °C–1.
solution_text: When the current through the element is very small, heating effects can be ignored and the temperature T 1 of the element is the same as room temperature. When the toaster is connected to the supply, its initial current will be slightly higher than its steady value of 2.68 A. But due to the heating effect of the current, the temperature will rise. This will cause an increase in resistance and a slight decrease in current. In a few seconds, a steady-state will be reached when the temperature will rise no further, and both the resistance of the element and the current drawn will achieve steady values. The resistance R 2 at the steady temperature T 2 is {tex}R_{2}=\frac{230 \mathrm{V}}{2.68 \mathrm{A}}=85.8 \Omega{/tex} Given T 1 = 27 o C. and resistance = 75.3 ohm Using the relation: R 2 = R 1 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T 2 – T 1 )] with {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 °C –1 , we get T 2 - T 1 = {tex}\frac{(85.8 \ - \ 75.3)}{(75.3) \ \times \ 1.70 \ \times \ 10^{-4}}=820^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex} that is, T 2 = (820 + 27.0) °C = 847 °C Thus, the steady temperature of the heating element (when heating effect due to the current equals heat loss to the surroundings) is given by 847 °C.
solution_html:
When the current through the element is very small, heating effects can be ignored and the temperature T1 of the element is the same as room temperature. When the toaster is connected to the supply, its initial current will be slightly higher than its steady value of 2.68 A. But due to the heating effect of the current, the temperature will rise. This will cause an increase in resistance and a slight decrease in current. In a few seconds, a steady-state will be reached when the temperature will rise no further, and both the resistance of the element and the current drawn will achieve steady values. The resistance R2 at the steady temperature T2 is {tex}R_{2}=\frac{230 \mathrm{V}}{2.68 \mathrm{A}}=85.8 \Omega{/tex}
Given T1 = 27oC. and  resistance = 75.3 ohm
Using the relation:
R2 = R1 [1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (T2 – T1)]
with {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4 °C–1, we get
T2 - T1 = {tex}\frac{(85.8 \ - \ 75.3)}{(75.3) \ \times \ 1.70 \ \times \ 10^{-4}}=820^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex}
that is, T2 = (820 + 27.0) °C = 847 °C
Thus, the steady temperature of the heating element (when heating effect due to the current equals heat loss to the surroundings) is given by  847 °C.

subject: Physics
question_text: The resistance of the platinum wire of a platinum resistance thermometer at the ice point is 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and at the steam point is 5.23 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . When the thermometer is inserted in a hot bath, the resistance of the platinum wire is 5.795 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Calculate the temperature of the bath.
question_html: The resistance of the platinum wire of a platinum resistance thermometer at the ice point is 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and at the steam point is 5.23 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. When the thermometer is inserted in a hot bath, the resistance of the platinum wire is 5.795 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Calculate the temperature of the bath.
solution_text: The resistance of a platinum wire at ice point R 0 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , The resistance of a platinum wire at steam point R 100 = 5.23 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and When the thermometer is inserted in a hot bath the resistance of the platinum wire is R t = 5.795 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Now, {tex}t=\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{R_{100}-R_{0}} \times 100{/tex} , R t = R 0 (1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} t) {tex}=\frac{5.795-5}{5.23-5} \times 100{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.795}{0.23} \times 100=345.65^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
The resistance of a platinum wire at ice point R0 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex},
The resistance of a platinum wire at steam point R100 = 5.23 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and
When the thermometer is inserted in a hot bath the resistance of the platinum wire is Rt = 5.795 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Now, {tex}t=\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{R_{100}-R_{0}} \times 100{/tex}, Rt = R0(1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}t)
{tex}=\frac{5.795-5}{5.23-5} \times 100{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.795}{0.23} \times 100=345.65^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A battery of 10 V and negligible internal resistance is connected across the diagonally opposite corners of a cubical network consisting of 12 resistors each of resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} (Fig.). Determine the equivalent resistance of the network and the current along each edge of the cube.
question_html: A battery of 10 V and negligible internal resistance is connected across the diagonally opposite corners of a cubical network consisting of 12 resistors each of resistance 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} (Fig.). Determine the equivalent resistance of the network and the current along each edge of the cube.
solution_text: The network is not reducible to a simple series and parallel combinations of resistors. There is, however, a clear symmetry in the problem which we can exploit to obtain the equivalent resistance of the network. The paths AA′, AD and AB are obviously symmetrically placed in the network. Thus, the current in each must be the same, say, I. Further, at the corners A′, B and D, the incoming current I must split equally into the two outgoing branches. In this manner, the current in all the 12 edges of the cube are easily written down in terms of I, using Kirchhoff’s first rule and the symmetry in the problem. Next take a closed loop, say, ABCC′EA, and apply Kirchhoff’s second rule: –IR – (1/2)IR – IR + {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} = 0 (Let E be EMF of cell. Due to symmetry of the cube, we can say that same current I flows through all the resistors.) where R is the resistance of each edge and {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} the emf of battery. Thus, {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{5}{2} I R{/tex} The equivalent resistance R eq of the network is {tex}R_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon}{3 I}=\frac{5}{6} R{/tex} For R = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R eq = ( {tex}\frac 56{/tex} ) {tex}\Omega{/tex} and for {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} = 10 V, the total current (= 3I) in the network is 3I = 10 V/( {tex}\frac 56{/tex} ) {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 12 A, i.e., I = 4 A
solution_html:
The network is not reducible to a simple series and parallel combinations of resistors. There is, however, a clear symmetry in the problem which we can exploit to obtain the equivalent resistance of the network. The paths AA′, AD and AB are obviously symmetrically placed in the network. Thus, the current in each must be the same, say, I. Further, at the corners A′, B and D, the incoming current I must split equally into the two outgoing branches. In this manner, the current in all the 12 edges of the cube are easily written down in terms of I, using Kirchhoff’s first rule and the symmetry in the problem. Next take a closed loop, say, ABCC′EA, and apply Kirchhoff’s second rule:
–IR – (1/2)IR – IR + {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} = 0 (Let E be EMF of cell. Due to symmetry of the cube, we can say that same current I flows through all the resistors.)
where R is the resistance of each edge and {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} the emf of battery. Thus,
{tex}\varepsilon=\frac{5}{2} I R{/tex}
The equivalent resistance Req of the network is {tex}R_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon}{3 I}=\frac{5}{6} R{/tex}
For R = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, Req
= ({tex}\frac 56{/tex}) {tex}\Omega{/tex} and for {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} = 10 V, the total current (= 3I) in the network is 3I = 10 V/({tex}\frac 56{/tex}) {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 12 A, i.e., I = 4 A

subject: Physics
question_text: Determine the current in each branch of the network shown in Fig.
question_html: Determine the current in each branch of the network shown in Fig.
solution_text: Each branch of the network is assigned an unknown current to be determined by the application of Kirchhoff’s rules. To reduce the number of unknowns at the outset, the first rule of Kirchhoff is used at every junction to assign the unknown current in each branch. (current entering into the junction is equal to current leaving the junction) We then have three unknowns I 1 , I 2 and I 3 which can be found by applying the second rule of Kirchhoff to three different closed loops. Kirchhoff’s second rule for the closed-loop ADCA gives, 10 – 4(I 1 – I 2 ) + 2(I 2 + I 3 – I 1 ) – I 1 = 0 ...(i) that is, 7I 1 – 6I 2 – 2I 3 = 10 For the closed-loop ABCA, we get 10 – 4I 2 – 2 (I 2 + I 3 ) – I 1 = 0 that is, I 1 + 6I 2 + 2I 3 = 10 ...(ii) For the closed-loop BCDEB, we get 5 – 2 (I 2 + I 3 ) – 2 (I 2 + I 3 – I 1 ) = 0 that is, 2I 1 – 4I 2 – 4I 3 = –5 ...(iii) Equations (i, ii, iii) are three simultaneous equations in three unknowns. These can be solved by the usual method to give I 1 = 2.5A, I 2 = {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} A, I 3 = 1 {tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex} A The currents in the various branches of the network are AB : {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} A, CA : {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} A, DEB : {tex}1 \frac{7}{8}{/tex} A AD : {tex}1 \frac{7}{8}{/tex} A, CD : 0 A, BC : {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} A It is easily verified that Kirchhoff’s second rule applied to the remaining closed loops does not provide any additional independent equation, that is, the above values of currents satisfy the second rule for every closed loop of the network. For example, the total voltage drop over the closed-loop BADEB {tex}5 \mathrm{V}+\left(\frac{5}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}-\left(\frac{15}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}{/tex} equal to zero, as required by Kirchhoff’s second rule.
solution_html:
Each branch of the network is assigned an unknown current to be determined by the application of Kirchhoff’s rules. To reduce the number of unknowns at the outset, the first rule of Kirchhoff is used at every junction to assign the unknown current in each branch. (current entering into the junction is equal to current leaving the junction) We then have three unknowns I1, I2 and I3 which can be found by applying the second rule of Kirchhoff to three different closed loops. Kirchhoff’s second rule for the closed-loop ADCA gives,
10 – 4(I1 – I2) + 2(I2 + I3 – I1) – I1 = 0 ...(i)
that is, 7I1 – 6I2 – 2I3 = 10
For the closed-loop ABCA, we get
10 – 4I2 – 2 (I2 + I3) – I1 = 0
that is, I1 + 6I2 + 2I3 = 10 ...(ii)
For the closed-loop BCDEB, we get
5 – 2 (I2 + I3) – 2 (I2 + I3 – I1) = 0
that is, 2I1 – 4I2 – 4I3 = –5 ...(iii)
Equations (i, ii, iii) are three simultaneous equations in three unknowns. These can be solved by the usual method to give
I1 = 2.5A, I2 = {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} A, I3 = 1{tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex} A
The currents in the various branches of the network are 
AB : {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex}A, CA : {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} A, DEB : {tex}1 \frac{7}{8}{/tex} A
AD : {tex}1 \frac{7}{8}{/tex}A, CD : 0 A, BC : {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} A
It is easily verified that Kirchhoff’s second rule applied to the remaining closed loops does not provide any additional independent equation, that is, the above values of currents satisfy the second rule for every closed loop of the network. For example, the total voltage drop over the closed-loop BADEB
{tex}5 \mathrm{V}+\left(\frac{5}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}-\left(\frac{15}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}{/tex} equal to zero, as required by Kirchhoff’s second rule.

subject: Physics
question_text: The four arms of a Wheatstone bridge (Fig.) have the following resistances: AB = 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , BC = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , CD = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , and DA = 60 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . A galvanometer of 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance is connected across BD. Calculate the current through the galvanometer when a potential difference of 10 V is maintained across AC.
question_html:
The four arms of a Wheatstone bridge (Fig.) have the following resistances:
AB = 100{tex}\Omega{/tex}, BC = 10{tex}\Omega{/tex}, CD = 5{tex}\Omega{/tex}, and DA = 60{tex}\Omega{/tex}.

A galvanometer of 15{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance is connected across BD. Calculate the current through the galvanometer when a potential difference of 10 V is maintained across AC.
solution_text: Applying kirchoff law: Considering the loop BADB, we have 100I 1 + 15I g – 60I 2 = 0 or 20I 1 + 3I g – 12I 2 = 0 [(a)] Considering the loop BCDB, we have 10 (I 1 – I g ) – 15I g – 5 (I 2 + I g ) = 0 10I 1 – 30I g –5I 2 = 0 2I 1 – 6I g – I 2 = 0 [(b)] Considering the loop ADCEA, 60I 2 + 5 (I 2 + I g ) = 10 65I 2 + 5I g = 10 13I 2 + I g = 2 [(c)] Multiplying Eq. (b) by 10 20I 1 – 60I g – 10I 2 = 0 [(d)] From Eqs. (d) and (a) we have 63I g – 2I 2 = 0 I 2 = 31.5I g [(e)] Substituting the value of I 2 into Eq. [(c)], we get 13 (31.5I g ) + I g = 2 410.5 I g = 2 I g = 4.87 mA.
solution_html:
Applying kirchoff law:
Considering the loop BADB, we have
100I1 + 15Ig – 60I2 = 0
or 20I1 + 3Ig – 12I2 = 0 [(a)]
Considering the loop BCDB, we have
10 (I1 – Ig) – 15Ig – 5 (I2 + Ig) = 0
10I1 – 30Ig –5I2 = 0
2I1 – 6Ig – I2 = 0 [(b)]
Considering the loop ADCEA,
60I2 + 5 (I2 + Ig) = 10
65I2 + 5Ig = 10
13I2 + Ig = 2 [(c)]
Multiplying Eq. (b) by 10
20I1 – 60Ig – 10I2 = 0 [(d)]
From Eqs. (d) and (a) we have
63Ig– 2I2 = 0
I2= 31.5Ig [(e)]
Substituting the value of I2 into Eq. [(c)], we get
13 (31.5Ig) + Ig= 2
410.5 Ig = 2
Ig= 4.87 mA.

subject: Physics
question_text: The storage battery of a car has an emf of 12 V. If the internal resistance of the battery is {tex}0.4\Omega {/tex} , what is the maximum current that can be drawn from the battery?
question_html: The storage battery of a car has an emf of 12 V. If the internal resistance of the battery is {tex}0.4\Omega {/tex}, what is the maximum current that can be drawn from the battery?
solution_text: When the external resistance in the circuit is zero i.e. R = 0, then the maximum current is drawn from a battery. Given, {tex}\varepsilon = 12V,\;r = 0.4{/tex} {tex}{I_{\max }} = \frac{\varepsilon }{r} = \frac{{12}}{{0.4}} = 30A{/tex}
solution_html:
When the external resistance in the circuit is zero i.e. R = 0, then the maximum current is drawn from a battery.
Given, {tex}\varepsilon = 12V,\;r = 0.4{/tex}
{tex}{I_{\max }} = \frac{\varepsilon }{r} = \frac{{12}}{{0.4}} = 30A{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A battery of emf 10 V and internal resistance {tex}3\Omega {/tex} is connected to a resistor. If the current in the circuit is 0.5 A, what is the resistance of the resistor? What is the terminal voltage of the battery when the circuit is closed?
question_html: A battery of emf 10 V and internal resistance {tex}3\Omega {/tex} is connected to a resistor. If the current in the circuit is 0.5 A, what is the resistance of the resistor? What is the terminal voltage of the battery when the circuit is closed?
solution_text: Since, {tex}I = \frac{\varepsilon }{{R + r}}{/tex} or {tex}R = \frac{\varepsilon }{I} - r{/tex} Putting given values {tex}\varepsilon = 10V,r = 3\Omega {/tex} and I = 0.5 A {tex}R = \frac{{10}}{{0.5}} - 3 = 17\Omega {/tex} Terminal voltage {tex}V = IR = 0.5 \times 17 = 8.5V{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, {tex}I = \frac{\varepsilon }{{R + r}}{/tex}
or {tex}R = \frac{\varepsilon }{I} - r{/tex}
Putting given values {tex}\varepsilon = 10V,r = 3\Omega {/tex} and I = 0.5 A
{tex}R = \frac{{10}}{{0.5}} - 3 = 17\Omega {/tex}
Terminal voltage {tex}V = IR = 0.5 \times 17 = 8.5V{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: At room temperature (27.0°C) the resistance of a heating element is {tex}100\Omega {/tex} . What is the temperature of the element if the resistance is found to be {tex}117\Omega {/tex} . Given that the temperature co-efficient of the material of the resistor is {tex}1.70 \times {10^{ - 4}}\;^\circ {C^{ - 1}}{/tex} .
question_html: At room temperature (27.0°C) the resistance of a heating element is {tex}100\Omega {/tex}. What is the temperature of the element if the resistance is found to be {tex}117\Omega {/tex}. Given that the temperature co-efficient of the material of the resistor is {tex}1.70 \times {10^{ - 4}}\;^\circ {C^{ - 1}}{/tex}.
solution_text: Given, R t = 117 ohm, R 27 = 100 ohm Since, {tex}{R_t} = {R_{27}}\left[ {1 + \alpha \left( {t - 27} \right)} \right]{/tex} Putting values, {tex}117 = 100\left[ {1 + 1.70 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}\left( {t - 27} \right)} \right]{/tex} t = 1000 + 27 = 1027°C
solution_html:
Given, Rt = 117 ohm, R27 = 100 ohm
Since, {tex}{R_t} = {R_{27}}\left[ {1 + \alpha \left( {t - 27} \right)} \right]{/tex}
Putting values, {tex}117 = 100\left[ {1 + 1.70 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}\left( {t - 27} \right)} \right]{/tex}
t = 1000 + 27 = 1027°C

subject: Physics
question_text: A negligibly small current is passed through a wire of length 15 m and uniform cross section {tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}{m^2}{/tex} , and its resistance is measured to be {tex}5.0\Omega {/tex} . What is the resistivity of the material at the temperature of the experiment?
question_html: A negligibly small current is passed through a wire of length 15 m and uniform cross section {tex}6.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}{m^2}{/tex}, and its resistance is measured to be {tex}5.0\Omega {/tex}. What is the resistivity of the material at the temperature of the experiment?
solution_text: Given, R = 5 ohm, l = 15 m {tex}A = 6 \times {10^{ - 7}}{m^2}{/tex} (Area of cross-section of wire) Resistivity {tex}\rho = \frac{{RA}}{l} = \frac{{5 \times 6.0 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{15}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}\Omega m{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, R = 5 ohm, l = 15 m
{tex}A = 6 \times {10^{ - 7}}{m^2}{/tex} (Area of cross-section of wire)
Resistivity {tex}\rho = \frac{{RA}}{l} = \frac{{5 \times 6.0 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{15}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}\Omega m{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A silver wire has a resistance of 2.1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at 27.5°C and a resistance of 2.7 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at 100°C. Determine the temperature co-efficient of resistivity of silver.
question_html: A silver wire has a resistance of 2.1{tex}\Omega{/tex} at 27.5°C and a resistance of 2.7{tex}\Omega{/tex} at 100°C. Determine the temperature co-efficient of resistivity of silver.
solution_html:
Given, R1 = 2.1{tex}\Omega{/tex}, t1 = 27.5oC, R2 = 2.7{tex}\Omega{/tex}, t2 = 100°C
Since R2 = R1[1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}(t2 - t1)]
{tex}\alpha = \frac{{{R_2} - {R_1}}}{{{R_1}({t_2} - {t_1})}} = \frac{{2.7 - 2.1}}{{2.1(100 - 27.5)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.6}}{{2.1 \times 72.5}} = \frac{{0.6}}{{152.25}} = 0.0039^\circ {C^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A heating element using nichrome connected to a 230 V supply draws an initial current of 3.2 A which settles after a few seconds to a steady value of 2.8 A. What is the steady temperature of the heating element if the room temperature is 27.0°C? Temperature co-efficient of resistance of nichrome averaged over the temperature range involved is {tex}1.70 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}{/tex} .
question_html: A heating element using nichrome connected to a 230 V supply draws an initial current of 3.2 A which settles after a few seconds to a steady value of 2.8 A. What is the steady temperature of the heating element if the room temperature is 27.0°C? Temperature co-efficient of resistance of nichrome averaged over the temperature range involved is {tex}1.70 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}{/tex}.
solution_html:
Here, {tex}{R_1} = \frac{{230}}{{3.2}} = 71.87\Omega {/tex}
{tex}{R_2} = \frac{{230}}{{2.8}} = 82.14\Omega {/tex}
{tex}\alpha = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}{/tex}
t1 = 27°C
Since R2 = R1[1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}(t2 - t1)]
Thus, {tex}{t_2} = \frac{{{R_2} - {R_1}}}{{{R_1} \cdot \alpha }} + {t_1}{/tex}
or {tex}{t_2} = \frac{{82.14 - 71.87}}{{71.87 \times 1.7 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}} + 27{/tex}
= 840.56 + 27
= 867.56°C
= 867°C

subject: Physics
question_text: Determine the current in each branch of the network shown in figure.
question_html: Determine the current in each branch of the network shown in figure.
solution_text: Applying Kirchhoff's law to the mesh ABDA, -10 1 - 5 g + (I - I 1 )5 = 0 {tex}3{I_1} - I + {I_g} = 0{/tex} ....... (i) Again, applying Kirchhoff's second law to the mesh BDCB, - 5I g - 10(I - I 1 + I g ) + 5(I 1 - I g ) = 0 3I 1 - 2I - 4I g = 0 ...... (ii) Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the mesh ABCEA, -10I 1 - 5(I 1 - I g ) - 10I + 10 = 0 3I 1 + 2I - I g = 2 ....... (iii) Adding (i) and (iii), we get 6I 1 + I = 2 ....... (iv) Multiplying (i) by 4 and adding in (ii), we get 15I 1 - 6I = 0......(v) Solving equations (iv) and (v), we get {tex}{I_1} = \frac{4}{{17}}A{/tex} = 0.235 A So, current in branch AB is 0.235 A Putting the value of I 1 in equation (v) and simplifying, we get Total current, {tex}I = \frac{{10}}{{17}}{/tex} = 0.588 A Putting the values of I and I 1 in equation (iii) and simplifying, we get {tex}{I_g} = \frac{2}{{17}}A = - 0.118A{/tex} The negative sign indicates that the direction of current is opposite to that shown in figure above So current in branch BD is -0.118A Current in branch BC is (I 1 - I g ) i.e. {tex}\frac{4}{{17}} - \left( { - \frac{2}{{17}}} \right){/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac{6}{{17}}{/tex} or 0.353 A Current in branch AD is (I - I 1 ) i.e. {tex}\left( {\frac{{10}}{{17}} - \frac{4}{{17}}} \right)A{/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac{6}{{17}}A{/tex} or 0.353 A Current in branch DC is (I - I 1 + I g ) i.e. {tex}\frac{6}{{17}} + \left( { - \frac{2}{{17}}} \right)A{/tex} or {tex}\frac{4}{{17}}A{/tex} or 0.235 A
solution_html:
Applying Kirchhoff's law to the mesh ABDA,
-101 - 5g + (I - I1)5 = 0
{tex}3{I_1} - I + {I_g} = 0{/tex}....... (i)

Again, applying Kirchhoff's second law to the mesh BDCB,
- 5Ig - 10(I - I1 + Ig) + 5(I1 - Ig) = 0
3I1 - 2I - 4Ig = 0 ...... (ii)
Applying Kirchhoff's second law to the mesh ABCEA,
-10I1 - 5(I1 - Ig) - 10I + 10 = 0
3I1 + 2I - Ig = 2 ....... (iii)
Adding (i) and (iii), we get
6I1 + I = 2 ....... (iv)
Multiplying (i) by 4 and adding in (ii), we get
15I1 - 6I = 0......(v)
Solving equations (iv) and (v), we get
{tex}{I_1} = \frac{4}{{17}}A{/tex} = 0.235 A
So, current in branch AB is 0.235 A
Putting the value of I1 in equation (v) and simplifying, we get
Total current, {tex}I = \frac{{10}}{{17}}{/tex} = 0.588 A
Putting the values of I and I1 in equation (iii) and simplifying, we get
{tex}{I_g} = \frac{2}{{17}}A = - 0.118A{/tex}
The negative sign indicates that the direction of current is opposite to that shown in figure above
So current in branch BD is -0.118A
Current in branch BC is (I1 - Ig) i.e. {tex}\frac{4}{{17}} - \left( { - \frac{2}{{17}}} \right){/tex}
i.e. {tex}\frac{6}{{17}}{/tex} or 0.353 A
Current in branch AD is (I - I1)
i.e. {tex}\left( {\frac{{10}}{{17}} - \frac{4}{{17}}} \right)A{/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac{6}{{17}}A{/tex} or 0.353 A
Current in branch DC is (I - I1 + Ig)
i.e. {tex}\frac{6}{{17}} + \left( { - \frac{2}{{17}}} \right)A{/tex} or {tex}\frac{4}{{17}}A{/tex} or 0.235 A

subject: Physics
question_text: A storage battery of emf 8.0 V and internal resistance 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is being charged by a 120 V dc supply using a series resistor of 15.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . What is the terminal voltage of the battery during charging? What is the purpose of having a series resistor in the charging circuit?
question_html: A storage battery of emf 8.0 V and internal resistance 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is being charged by a 120 V dc supply using a series resistor of 15.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. What is the terminal voltage of the battery during charging? What is the purpose of having a series resistor in the charging circuit?
solution_text: Electromotive force EMF of battery = 8 Volt Internal resistance of battery r = 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Supply Voltage V = 120 Volt Resistance of resistor R = 15.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Effective voltage in circuit = V1 Since Resistance R is connected in series. Hence, we can write V 1 = V - E V 1 = 120V - 8V = 112 V Current flowing in the circuit: {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{V_{1}}{R+r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{112 \mathrm{V}}{15.5 \Omega \ + \ 0.5 \mathrm{\Omega}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{112 \mathrm{V}}{16 \Omega}{/tex} I = 7 Ampere By Ohm’s Law, Voltage across resistor R is given by V = IR V = 7A {tex}\times {/tex} 15.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = 108.5 Volt Supply Voltage = Terminal Voltage of battery + Voltage drop across Resistor R Therefore Terminal Voltage of battery = 120 V - 108.5 V = 11.5 Volt The series resistor in the charging circuit reduces the current drawn from the external supply and the current will be too high in its absence. Series resistor in the charging circuit limits the current drawn from the external source.
solution_html:
Electromotive force EMF of battery = 8 Volt
Internal resistance of battery r = 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Supply Voltage V = 120 Volt
Resistance of resistor R = 15.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Effective voltage in circuit = V1
Since Resistance R is connected in series. Hence, we can write V1 = V - E
V1 = 120V - 8V = 112 V
Current flowing in the circuit:
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{V_{1}}{R+r}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{112 \mathrm{V}}{15.5 \Omega \ + \ 0.5 \mathrm{\Omega}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{112 \mathrm{V}}{16 \Omega}{/tex}
I = 7 Ampere
By Ohm’s Law, Voltage across resistor R is given by V = IR
V = 7A {tex}\times {/tex} 15.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}V = 108.5 Volt
Supply Voltage = Terminal Voltage of battery + Voltage drop across Resistor R
Therefore Terminal Voltage of battery = 120 V - 108.5 V = 11.5 Volt
The series resistor in the charging circuit reduces the current drawn from the external supply and the current will be too high in its absence. Series resistor in the charging circuit limits the current drawn from the external source.

subject: Physics
question_text: The number density of free electrons in a copper conductor is 8.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 m -3 . How long does an electron take to drift from one end of a wire 3.0 m long to its other end? The area of cross section of the wire is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m 2 and it is carrying a current of 3.0 A.
question_html: The number density of free electrons in a copper conductor is 8.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 m-3 . How long does an electron take to drift from one end of a wire 3.0 m long to its other end? The area of cross section of the wire is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 m2 and it is carrying a current of 3.0 A.
solution_text: n = 8.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 m -3 ; I = 3.0 A A = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m 2 , l = 3.0 m, e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C Drift velocity, {tex}{v_d} = \frac{I}{{neA}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{3}{{8.5 \times {{10}^{28}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{/tex} = 1.103 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ms -1 {tex}t = \frac{l}{{{v_d}}} = \frac{{3.0}}{{1.103 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{/tex} = 2.72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 s ( {tex}\approx{/tex} 7.5 h)
solution_html:
n = 8.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 m-3; I = 3.0 A
A = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 m2 , l = 3.0 m, e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C
Drift velocity, {tex}{v_d} = \frac{I}{{neA}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{3}{{8.5 \times {{10}^{28}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{/tex}
= 1.103 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 ms-1
{tex}t = \frac{l}{{{v_d}}} = \frac{{3.0}}{{1.103 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{/tex} = 2.72 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 s ({tex}\approx{/tex} 7.5 h)
